speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this-- this picture shows my father, probably around 1929, on-- on a vacation in, I believe, in Italy. And-- that-- that-- you know. This was-- Yeah, this is my mother, Herta Summerfeld. I-- I believe it's-- she sitting in one of those beach chairs on the German bathing resort of Osmonde. In-- during-- probably taken around 1929, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this picture depicts us-- depicts my brother and I with friends in Silesia in about 1935, '38. I'm on the right in the lederhosen. My brother is next over in a Bavarian outfit, is second from-- from right. The two other boys are Hans and Gerhard. These were the sons of the couple that's standing behind them. They were friends of ours. Mr. And Mrs. Joseph Trembau. And the young lady on the left is-- was Gertrud Seidelmann She was our-- our maid. That picture, yeah, that picture was taken on vacation.

speaker: interviewer
text: These are the couple that you stayed with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stayed in Silesia when I was-- yeah, they-- they-- no, they-- they were the people that provided us with-- with housing when we were bombed out and had to move to Silesia. This picture shows the sanatorium in Berchtesgaden on the Obersalzberg, where Hitler had his Eagle's Nest. This-- my father had this as a convalescent home for many years up until 1933, '34. Then it was taken over, confiscated by the Nazi government that established houses, retreat places for all their top officers in the-- on the Eagle's Nest mountain. Yeah, in the winter of 1948, I was spending about three months in a Joint Distribution Agency one-- rest home in the Bavarian Alps. I was already working as a reporter, and I had to take off this time. So I-- I also took-- I also took my car with me and my press sign. And this shows me getting stuck in the snow because I strayed off the main highway. And I'm--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of my friends from the rest home, from the agency rest home took the picture. This shows the picture-- this the castle-- the sanatorium where I spent about three months for my suspected TB, which was run way up in the mountains, the Bavarian Alps, with stacks of, piles of snow. This castle, this rest home was run by the Joint Distribution Agency in cooperation with the-- United Nations Relief Organization. And it was designed for getting people back to health that are survivors of the extermination camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: This shows my complete family of eight-- seven girls and one son. And I identify them from left to right. That's Trisha, first one, Christine, Suzanne, Debbie, Mary Kate, Karen, Peggy, and Thomas. You see this-- See? I knew I would. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, now I would like to introduce my family. And to my right-- to my right is my wife, Joy. Next to her is my daughter, Suzanne, and her daughter, Shannon. Next to her-- I can't even-- is Maggie, who-- and who's also her daughter, Suzanne's daughter. And next to her is my-- my daughter, Christine. And then we go down and back from-- from behind me is my daughter, Trisha, and her child, Michelle. And then there's Debbie. And this is Anthony, who belongs to Trisha. He's my grandson. And then there is Diana sitting in back, nice and pretty on the chair. That's Christine's daughter. And there is Christopher, who is Suzanne's son. And there is my son-in-law, John and my-- my son's and daughter Christine's son, Jamie, at the far end. So now that I have introduced everybody, I would like to say something to everybody and see how they-- how everything is-- how everybody feels today. I-- I'm very-- I'm very much surprised that we can keep them all quiet. This is actually not-- it's actually not the actual number of all the people. We get the immediate family together, it would be 23 people. So we're-- we're missing a good deal. We'll see their picture in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you'll see the picture in the-- in the-- in the-- on the monitor. Now--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not unusual for Dad to mess names because with eight children, all of our lives, he went through about two or three names before he got the one he wanted.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi, hi, sweetheart.

speaker: interviewer
text: When who was a teenager?

speaker: interviewee
text: Papa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you know the story about Papa being stuck in the-- could you tell us about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: You can tell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I forget. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: We did talk about that. I do want to say that it's-- there will be very, very few people that you know or meet in your lifetime that, you know, had to-- have had to endure such horrible and incredible experiences that you can, you know, barely even imagine what it was like, and you know, amazingly, my father is one of those people. And I-- I know that we're all very, very proud of him and admire him for that. It's incredible to think that he came to this country with just, you know, a couple dollars in his pocket and was able to achieve this family and this life and-- and a successful career. And I-- I think that we all hope that the strength and determination and courage that he used to survive those experiences, you know, during the war are qualities that we will be able to bring to our own lives, and-- and qualities that our children will be able to draw upon when they have their own life experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Christine, you want to say--

speaker: interviewee
text: I would just like to say that I am very, very happy that this whole situation has taken place, because since I first met him, I have been asking him to write a book or write a story telling about this, putting it all down so he didn't forget it. And he's always been going to do it, and he never has, and actually, had spoken so little of it to the children that we did have to rake up an old article for them to read to make sure they really knew-- not all of them, but some of them, the younger ones-- that they really knew what he had been through. So it hasn't been a main topic of conversation, and I think I was afraid it would be lost. So I'm very, very happy about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Christine, you want to say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we're just so proud of him. We're just really happy that he was able to come here and just be as successful as he has been. And just-- I mean, we're just-- it's a miracle that he survived, and if he didn't survive, none of us would be here. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We'd be missing a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there's a lot of us now. So we're really happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: If someone would have told me, you know, that I would have a family of 23 when I came over by myself, one person, I would have thought that person is nuts.

speaker: interviewee
text: You would have got on a boat and gone back. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I-- I couldn't take. That would-- that's just too much. John, you want to say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just coming into the family, I consider myself very lucky. My background, knowing the history, and studying the history, and realizing what a horrible time, not really having personal experiences or-- or relatives who were directly involved in such a-- a magnificent way. Actually, I find myself very fortunate because of his fortitude and strength, being able to survive that and come over here. I lucked out because I was able to marry his daughter, and so my legacy is directly because of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, John.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, did you want to say something, dear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sweetheart?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell about the story that you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's good that he came here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to say something, Debbie?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm just very grateful that you're here and that we are all here with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good. Debbie?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would just like to say that I consider my dad a [INAUDIBLE]. It's almost incomprehensible to-- to imagine. I think I was about 13 years old when I first realized what he had gone through. But-- it's-- really, it hurts me to think that anyone would have had to go through anything like that. And I absolutely do believe it-- all of us have truly been blessed twice by God, not only with the Saint Joseph prayer, but having the last name of Joseph, and that it's-- it's a-- a miracle that if this one person didn't survive, that I wouldn't be here today, or any of us. And I hope that-- that the-- the joy that his family and his children and his grandchildren bring him can somehow make-- make it up to him. I think that it's important that if things go wrong, in a way that it's-- it's good that people are able to make things up to people, that somehow that-- that can help you get through and move on. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I just wanted to say that I'm very proud of my father, and I appreciate everything he's ever been through. And I think it's important that we all remember what he's gone through and others like him, and that our children know and we remember this. So just to hope that it doesn't happen again, but, you know, unfortunately, things like this do happen. But I just think that it's always important to remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Trisha.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think maybe we can close now.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to hear from you. You have some things to say that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, well, yeah, no, but I think, just as a closing remark, I-- I-- like I said earlier in my-- in interviews. I still have some, you know, uneasy feelings when I go back to Germany, because I can't forget these times, that we-- the fear and the persecution and humiliation. And a lot of times when I'm traveling to see my brother in Germany, I feel that people see of my generation, I wonder what were they doing between 1933 and 1945? Now, I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it-- it-- it was such a-- Holocaust, such an uproar. And I think my-- my final comment would be we can never let the forces of denial, which do exist, that the Holocaust was not-- was not existing, can never let them to be expressed. That would be wrong, because I think we would do a great injustice to all those millions of people, our brothers and sisters, that were murdered in the-- in the concentration camps. We must continue to go on. Maybe some of us can forget, can forgive, but-- but nobody can ever forget what happened to us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much, sir. Was there more that you had wanted to say? No, that's-- that's-- yeah, I'd like to thank you very much for the opportunity to be interviewed for this-- for this documentary. Thank you. Thanks all of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: When are we going to take a picture? [LAUGHTER]
